item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Computing's Breakout Moment
item.description:
The quantum computing world is experiencing what may be its most consequential stretch yet, with commercial milestones, record investment, and fundamental physics discoveries all converging at once.
Let's start with the biggest industry development. Quantinuum launched its Helios quantum computer in November, calling it the most accurate commercial system available. The company's lead architect, Anthony Ransford, made the striking claim that you'd need to harvest every star in the universe to p...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Infrastructure Race Heats Up
item.description: Today in quantum computing, there are significant moves on the infrastructure and investment front. AWS is expanding its quantum research presence in Pasadena, California, purchasing a 168,000 square foot facility at 2964 Bradley Street for roughly $78 million — more than double what the property sold for in 2021. This is separate from AWS's existing quantum computing center on the Caltech campus, which launched in 2021 and spans about 21,000 square feet focused on developing fault-tolerant quan...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Meets Real-World Operations
item.description: The most striking quantum development today is a real-world proof point: D-Wave's hybrid quantum annealing system has cut emergency response times for North Wales Police by nearly 50 percent. The system solved what's called the forward deployment problem — optimally positioning police vehicles across ten local teams spanning diverse geography — compressing a task that previously took four months of classical computation into just four minutes. The project achieved 90 percent of target response t...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Hardware and Infrastructure Advances
item.description:
Several major developments are reshaping the quantum computing landscape today. Let's start with the hardware breakthroughs.
Microsoft has formally introduced Majorana 1, the world's first quantum chip powered by what it calls a Topological Core architecture. The chip leverages a new class of material — a topoconductor made from indium arsenide and aluminum, fabricated atom by atom — that can coax exotic Majorana particles into existence. These particles hide quantum information, making it inhe...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Partnerships and Supercomputing Architecture
item.description:
Today's quantum news centers on major partnerships and a significant architectural milestone that collectively signal the field's accelerating move from lab curiosity to practical infrastructure.
The biggest story is IBM's articulation of a reference architecture for quantum-centric supercomputing — essentially a blueprint for integrating quantum processors into existing high-performance computing data centers. The framing from IBM, and echoed in Barron's coverage, is notable: quantum isn't jus...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Investment Meets National Security Controls
item.description:
Today's quantum briefing centers on a rapidly intensifying dynamic at the intersection of quantum technology investment and national security regulation. A detailed analysis from Freshfields lays out how governments are playing both sides of the quantum coin — pouring billions into developing national quantum champions while simultaneously erecting protective barriers to ensure rivals can't benefit from those advances.
The core issue is quantum technology's dual-use nature. Policymakers increas...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Error Correction and Internet Advances
item.description:
Today's quantum landscape is dominated by two converging themes: the push toward a quantum internet and the accelerating race to solve quantum error correction. Let's start with the biggest developments.
Quantinuum has demonstrated remarkable results on its 98-qubit Helios trapped-ion processor using what are called iceberg codes. They achieved 94 error-detected logical qubits and 48 error-corrected logical qubits from just 98 physical qubits — a near 2-to-1 physical-to-logical ratio for error ...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Nvidia's Quantum and AI Expansion
item.description:
Today in quantum and adjacent computing news, two stories highlight different ends of the spectrum — from a Danish startup working at the frontier of quantum chemistry to Nvidia's continued dominance and expanding reach into quantum computing infrastructure.
Let's start with Nvidia, which remains arguably the most consequential technology company of this decade. The company just posted record fiscal fourth-quarter revenue of $68.1 billion, driven by its data center division which alone brought ...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Fault-Tolerant Quantum Architectures Advance
item.description: The biggest development today in quantum computing is a new partnership between Paris-based C12 and Germany's QC Design. C12 is adopting QC Design's Plaquette design-automation platform to accelerate its path toward fault-tolerant quantum computing. C12's approach is distinctive — they use isotopically purified carbon-12 nanotubes suspended above silicon chips to host spin qubits. This technique maximally isolates qubits from environmental noise, yielding coherence times two orders of magnitude ...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - UK Bets Big on Quantum Future
item.description: The biggest quantum news today centers on the United Kingdom making an enormous bet on quantum computing. Chancellor Rachel Reeves and Technology Secretary Liz Kendall announced up to two billion pounds — roughly two and a half billion US dollars — to scale quantum computers on British soil by the early 2030s. The centerpiece is a world-first one billion pound procurement program where the government will actually buy quantum computers from the first UK companies to build them at commercial scal...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Turing Award and Quantum-Classical Integration
item.description: The biggest news today is that Charles H. Bennett and Gilles Brassard have been awarded the 2025 ACM Turing Award — the so-called Nobel Prize of Computing — carrying a one-million-dollar prize supported by Google. Bennett, an 82-year-old IBM fellow, and Brassard, a 70-year-old professor at the University of Montreal, are recognized as the founders of quantum information science. Their story begins with a chance meeting at a conference in Puerto Rico in 1979, where Bennett reportedly approached B...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Earnings, Cryptography, and Workforce
item.description: The biggest story in quantum today is the financial picture emerging from Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings for the three publicly traded quantum computing firms. IonQ stands out as the clear revenue leader, posting $130 million in full-year GAAP revenue — a 202 percent increase over FY24 — making it the first public quantum company to cross the $100 million annual revenue threshold. Its Q4 alone brought in nearly $62 million, up 429 percent year-over-year. IonQ is projecting $225 to $245 million i...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Software Goes Public on Nasdaq
item.description: The biggest quantum news today is the Nasdaq debut of Horizon Quantum Computing, an Irish-founded company that builds software development tools designed to unlock the potential of quantum hardware. The listing came through a SPAC merger with dMY Squared Technology Group, netting Horizon roughly $120 million in gross proceeds before transaction expenses. CEO and founder Dr. Joe Fitzsimons, who has more than 20 years in quantum computing research, said the company will channel those funds into ac...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Error Modeling, Silicon Qubits, UK Quantum Funding
item.description: The biggest technical story today comes from Sandia National Laboratories, where Jordan Hines and colleagues have developed a new simulation technique that accounts for realistic error types in quantum circuits — specifically coherent and non-Pauli errors that have been largely ignored in fault-tolerant quantum computing analysis. The results are sobering: coherent errors can cause logical failure rates more than eight times higher than the simplified Pauli stochastic models the field has been r...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Ecosystems, Chips, and Policy Push
item.description: Today's quantum landscape is defined by three converging forces: regional ecosystem building, hardware breakthroughs from tech giants, and growing government engagement. Let's start with the biggest policy moves. California has launched Quantum California, a statewide public-private initiative under the California Jobs First Economic Blueprint — the state's first bottom-up economic strategy in more than 20 years. Leaders from science, industry, and the governor's office convened at UC Santa Barb...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Strategy, Chips, and Breakthroughs
item.description: Several important threads are converging in the quantum computing world right now. Let's start with policy. The Government Accountability Office just published a report flagging serious strategic gaps in the National Quantum Initiative Act ahead of a Senate Commerce Committee markup on its reauthorization this Wednesday. The GAO found that the current national quantum strategy lacks specific objectives for quantum computing, has no metrics to measure federal quantum activities, doesn't address f...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Google Expands Into Neutral Atom Quantum
item.description: The biggest news today is Google Quantum AI's announcement that it's expanding beyond its decade-long focus on superconducting qubits to embrace neutral atom quantum computing as a second modality. The logic is compelling: superconducting qubits already handle circuits with millions of gate cycles, each taking just a microsecond, making them strong in circuit depth. Neutral atoms, meanwhile, have scaled to arrays of roughly ten thousand qubits with flexible any-to-any connectivity, but their gat...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Infrastructure, Investment, and Breakthroughs
item.description:
Today's quantum news spans major developments in hardware strategy, networking breakthroughs, materials science, investment, and sustainability concerns.
Let's start with Google, which just announced a significant expansion of its quantum roadmap. After more than a decade focused exclusively on superconducting qubits, Google Quantum AI is now adding neutral atom qubits as a second modality. Hartmut Neven, the founder and lead of Google Quantum AI, said the move is about exploiting the complemen...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Encryption Threats and Market Reality
item.description: The biggest quantum computing story today is Google's stark warning that quantum computers could break most existing encryption systems by 2029. In a blog post, the Alphabet-owned company said encryption currently used to keep information confidential could easily be broken by a large-scale quantum computer in coming years, and urged banks, governments, and technology providers to begin migrating to post-quantum cryptography immediately. Google said it has already adjusted its own threat model t...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Xanadu IPO and Quantum Security Timeline
item.description:
The biggest quantum computing story today is Xanadu Quantum Technologies going public. The Toronto-based company began trading on both the TSX and Nasdaq on Friday under the ticker XNDU, making it the first Canadian tech company to debut on the Toronto Stock Exchange since 2021 and the first pure-play photonic quantum computing business to go public anywhere.
Xanadu achieved this through a deSPAC merger with Philadelphia-based Crane Harbor Acquisition Corp, a deal originally valued at roughly 3...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Race, Security, and Breakthroughs
item.description:
The quantum computing landscape is heating up on multiple fronts today, from geopolitics and cybersecurity to fundamental hardware breakthroughs and machine learning applications.
Let's start with the big picture. A new Jefferies report lays out the intensifying U.S.-China quantum computing race, and the numbers are striking. China has poured an estimated $16 billion in public funding into quantum — roughly four times what the U.S. government has invested. Beijing has embedded quantum computing...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Security, Cooling, and Industry Race
item.description: The biggest quantum news today centers on security. Google has drawn a firm line, setting 2029 as its deadline for a complete migration to post-quantum cryptography. Heather Adkins, VP of Security Engineering, framed it as a responsibility — Google leading by example to push the entire industry toward quantum-resistant encryption before powerful quantum machines can break what we rely on today. The specific threat driving this urgency is 'store-now-decrypt-later' attacks, where adversaries inter...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Infrastructure Battles and Breakthroughs
item.description: There's a major scientific breakthrough to lead with today. Researchers at the University of Central Florida have published a study in Science demonstrating a method for scaling up quantum entanglement using topologically protected modes of light in silicon photonic superlattices. Professor Andrea Blanco-Redondo's team managed to entangle three, four, and five topological modes by cleverly rearranging waveguide configurations on silicon chips — rather than making the system more elaborate. The k...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Breakthroughs: Fewer Qubits, Bigger Risks
item.description:
Today brings several significant developments in quantum computing, spanning hardware thresholds, security warnings, fundamental research, and real-world applications.
Perhaps the most striking headline comes from Caltech, where a research team has found that useful quantum computers could be built with as few as 10,000 qubits. That's a dramatically lower bar than many in the field had assumed, and it could reshape timelines for when quantum advantage becomes practically achievable.
That lower...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Encryption Threat Accelerates Urgently
item.description: The most consequential quantum computing news right now centers on encryption — specifically, how close we are to breaking it. Two research teams have just published stunning findings about the resources needed to crack elliptic curve cryptography, the encryption underpinning internet banking, cryptocurrency, and much of our digital security infrastructure. Dolev Bluvstein at Oratomic and his team believe it could fall to a quantum machine with just 10,000 qubits, using ultracold atom technology...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Encryption Threats Accelerate Dramatically
item.description:
The quantum computing world is reeling from what Scott Aaronson called 'quantum computing bombshells.' Two independent preprints posted on March 31st — one from Google, the other from Oratomic, a Caltech spin-off in Pasadena — both suggest that quantum computers capable of breaking current encryption could arrive before the end of this decade. That's far earlier than the at-least-ten-year timeline the cybersecurity community had been working with.
The Oratomic result is particularly striking. T...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Encryption Cracking Draws Nearer
item.description: Two major announcements have significantly narrowed the gap between theoretical quantum computing and the breaking of real-world encryption. A team at Caltech, led by physicists Dolev Bluvstein and Madelyn Cain along with colleagues Qian Xu, Robert Huang, Manuel Endres, and senior adviser John Preskill, has published a design for a neutral atom quantum computer that could crack widely used encryption schemes with far fewer qubits than anyone previously thought possible. Their key innovation: usi...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Computing Daily Update
item.description: Unfortunately, today's quantum computing briefing is light — the feed we pulled returned only a Google News landing page without any extractable article content. No substantive developments in quantum hardware, algorithms, industry partnerships, or policy were available to report from this source. We'll be back with a full briefing as soon as new content comes through....

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Computing: Scams and Breakthroughs
item.description:
Two very different stories in the quantum computing world today — one raising serious red flags about hype and fraud, the other marking genuine scientific progress.
Let's start with the cautionary tale. Xanadu, a Toronto-based quantum computing company trading under ticker XNDU, went public last month via a SPAC merger at roughly a three billion dollar valuation. The numbers are stark: about three million dollars in revenue against roughly sixty million in annual losses. Short-seller newsletter...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - IonQ's Accuracy Edge and Quantum Ecosystem Growth
item.description: Today in quantum computing, two stories highlight both the commercial race and the growing academic ecosystem shaping the field. First, IonQ continues to stand out among pure-play quantum computing companies, and the reason comes down to its trapped ion architecture. Unlike superconducting approaches, where qubits can only interact with their immediate neighbors, trapped ion technology allows every qubit in the system to communicate with every other qubit. That full connectivity translates into ...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Gains Ground Amid Market Caution
item.description: Let's start with the biggest research development today. A team from the University of Science and Technology of China, led by Professor Peng Xinhua, has published results in Physical Review Letters showing that a quantum processor with just nine interacting atomic spins outperformed classical neural networks containing thousands of nodes on real-world weather forecasting. The key innovation here is quantum reservoir computing — a brain-inspired approach where the natural dynamics of the quantum...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - AI Accelerates Quantum Encryption Threat
item.description: The biggest quantum computing story right now is a potential sea change in the encryption threat timeline. Researchers from Oratomic, a startup co-founded by Dolev Bluvstein, and Google have published new papers suggesting quantum computers capable of breaking internet encryption could arrive significantly sooner than expected. The reaction has been swift — Cloudflare is accelerating its post-quantum preparation deadline to 2029, and cybersecurity researcher Bas Westerbaan called it 'a real shoc...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Advances in Finance and Cryptography
item.description: Today in quantum, a few significant developments across both the commercial and research sides of the field. Let's start with the most consequential. Quanta Magazine is reporting that two independent research groups have made substantial progress in reducing the number of qubits and the time required to break common online encryption technologies. This is a big deal — it means the timeline for quantum computers posing a real threat to current security infrastructure may be shorter than many expe...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Ecosystems, Security, and Simulability
item.description: The biggest infrastructure story in quantum today is IQM Quantum Computers establishing its first US Quantum Technology Center at the University of Maryland's Discovery District. The Finnish company, which already operates quantum data centers in Finland and Germany and has delivered systems to supercomputing centers in South Korea, Poland, Italy, and Taiwan, chose College Park specifically for its proximity to NIST, NASA Goddard, Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory, and the broader federal...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Error Correction Race Heats Up
item.description: The quantum computing field saw several significant developments this week, centered on the persistent challenge of error correction and qubit stability. Alice & Bob announced a 160-fold improvement in error correction by introducing a technique called qubit squeezing to their cat qubits — the same approach used in AWS's Ocelot chip. Senior researcher Anil Murani said this brings them closer to what the industry calls escape velocity, the point where error rates stop growing exponentially as you...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Photonics Infrastructure Gains Momentum
item.description: Today's top quantum development centers on infrastructure — specifically the photonics hardware that underpins quantum computing, sensing, and networking platforms. Monarch Quantum, a US-based company founded just last year, announced a fifty-five million dollar oversubscribed growth round led by Serendipity Capital, with participation from 55 North and Global Innovation Labs. What makes this story striking is the speed and scale. Within six months of its founding, Monarch has accumulated more t...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - NVIDIA Launches AI-Powered Quantum Models
item.description:
NVIDIA made a significant move into quantum computing this week, announcing what it calls the world's first family of open-source AI models built specifically to accelerate practical quantum development. The new model family is called NVIDIA Ising, and it targets the two biggest bottlenecks facing the quantum industry: processor calibration and error correction.
The Ising Calibration model is a vision-language model that can interpret measurements from quantum processors and automate calibratio...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Computing Hits Inflection Points
item.description: The biggest quantum computing news right now is a convergence of breakthroughs that's dramatically compressing timelines. Let's start with what may be the most consequential development: a paper from Caltech and the startup Oratomic, described by computer scientist Scott Aaronson as a 'bombshell,' outlines a fault-tolerant quantum computing scheme that could reduce the required number of qubits by two orders of magnitude — down to roughly 10,000 instead of millions. Pair that with Google's recen...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Nvidia Ignites Quantum Computing Rally
item.description: It's been a landmark week for quantum computing, driven primarily by Nvidia's announcement of Ising — a new family of open-source AI models designed to tackle two of the hardest problems in the field: quantum error correction and calibration. Jensen Huang framed AI as the control plane, essentially the operating system, of quantum machines, saying it's what transforms fragile qubits into scalable, reliable quantum-GPU systems. The announcement dropped on World Quantum Day, April 14th, chosen bec...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Security Deadlines and AI Breakthroughs
item.description:
The quantum computing world is seeing a significant acceleration in urgency around post-quantum cryptography, alongside major new tools aimed at making quantum hardware actually useful. Let's start with the security story, which is arguably the most consequential.
Google and Cloudflare both moved their internal deadlines for full post-quantum cryptography readiness to 2029, about five years sooner than previously planned. The catalyst was two recent research papers that dramatically revised est...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Internet, SPACs, and Majorana States
item.description: The most striking quantum development today comes from the privacy and security front. Researchers have demonstrated that on a future quantum internet, VPNs would essentially be useless — your location can always be verified. The protocol uses entangled photons and a specific type of Bell test to perform quantum position verification. Here's how it works: a remote party measures properties of entangled photons simultaneously with the sender, and over multiple rounds, the Bell test confirms stron...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Distributed Quantum Computing Breakthroughs Emerge
item.description: The biggest quantum computing story right now is coming out of Oxford, where researchers have demonstrated something the field has been working toward for years: two separate quantum computers operating as a single machine through quantum gate teleportation. The team built two trapped-ion modules, nicknamed Alice and Bob, sitting about two meters apart. Rather than physically transferring quantum information between them, they used photons to create entanglement between the modules, then leverag...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum-AI Synergy and Hardware Advances
item.description:
The biggest theme in quantum computing right now is the growing evidence that even today's imperfect quantum hardware can deliver real, practical advantages — especially when paired with classical systems.
Researchers from University College London published findings in Science Advances showing they've achieved what they call practical quantum advantage by hooking up an AI model running on a supercomputer to a quantum computer at the Leibniz Supercomputing Centre in Germany. The setup predicted...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Reversing Time, Honors, and Quantum Strategy
item.description: Three significant developments in quantum computing today. First, theoretical physicists at Los Alamos National Laboratory have shown how to reverse the arrow of time in a quantum system. Luis Pedro García-Pintos and colleagues, publishing in Physical Review X, used computer simulations to demonstrate that by knowing a quantum system's original state and its post-measurement outcome, they could construct a control sequence — a Hamiltonian — that acts like Maxwell's demon, reverting the system ba...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Pennsylvania Launches AI Quantum Factory
item.description: Today in quantum computing news, Pennsylvania is making a notable move to establish itself as a hub for quantum and AI technology. Universities across the commonwealth, together with state leaders, have officially launched the Keystone AI and Quantum Factory — an initiative designed to bring together academic research and government support to accelerate practical applications of both AI and quantum computing. Details are still emerging, but the effort signals Pennsylvania's intent to compete fo...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Networking, Scaling, and Sensing Advances
item.description: The biggest quantum story today is Cisco's unveiling of its Universal Quantum Switch — a working prototype designed to connect quantum computers from different vendors and different modalities into a single network. Vijoy Pandey, SVP and GM of Cisco's Outshift incubation arm, framed the problem clearly: practical quantum applications will require hundreds of thousands to millions of qubits, but current systems operate at 100 to 1,000 qubits, with public roadmaps suggesting low tens of thousands ...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Cracks ECC, Markets React
item.description: The biggest quantum computing story today is a researcher successfully breaking a 15-bit elliptic curve cryptographic key on quantum hardware, winning Project Eleven's Q-Day Prize. Now, 15 bits is a far cry from the 256-bit keys that secure real-world cryptocurrency transactions, but the direction of progress is what matters here. Both Bitcoin and Ethereum rely on elliptic curve cryptography for transaction security, and an estimated $600 billion in Bitcoin currently sits in addresses with expos...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Quantum
Weekly Digest: Quantum
item.description:
This week in quantum computing, one overarching narrative dominates everything else: the field is crossing from experimental curiosity into something that looks increasingly like a real industry, with all the commercial partnerships, infrastructure buildouts, regulatory friction, and engineering milestones that come with that transition.
The week opened with a sweeping survey of the field's breakout moment. The numbers alone tell a striking story. Quantum startups raised nearly four billion dol...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Quantum
item.description:
This week in quantum computing has been one of the most eventful in recent memory, with developments spanning government policy, financial milestones, fundamental research, and the continued blurring of boundaries between quantum and classical computing. The overarching story is one of an industry decisively crossing from laboratory curiosity into industrial infrastructure, even as deep technical challenges remind us just how much work remains.
The most commanding theme this week has been the m...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Quantum
item.description:
This week in quantum computing, a remarkably coherent picture emerged of a field that is simultaneously maturing, fragmenting, and racing against its own implications. Three dominant themes wove through virtually every development: the urgent scramble to prepare for quantum's threat to encryption, a hardware landscape diversifying faster than ever, and a global competition for talent, investment, and strategic positioning that is reshaping national economic policy.
The most urgent and recurring...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Quantum
item.description:
This week in quantum computing has been dominated by a single, urgent realization that echoed across nearly every day of coverage: the timeline for quantum computers breaking modern encryption has collapsed far more rapidly than almost anyone anticipated, and the ripple effects are touching everything from cryptocurrency markets to national defense policy to how ordinary internet security works.
The bombshell came at the end of the week's midpoint. Two independent research teams, one from Calte...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Quantum
item.description:
This week in quantum computing offered a remarkably vivid portrait of a field caught between genuine scientific momentum and the persistent tensions of commercialization, hype, and hard-nosed engineering reality. Several threads wove through the week's developments, and together they tell a story about an industry that is maturing — sometimes painfully, sometimes impressively — on multiple fronts simultaneously.
The most dramatic storyline was the accelerating threat quantum computers pose to e...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Quantum
item.description:
This week in quantum computing has been one of those rare stretches where seemingly every corner of the field produced meaningful news, and the cumulative effect is a picture of a discipline approaching genuine inflection points on multiple fronts simultaneously.
The single most dominant theme is the relentless push toward solving quantum error correction and scalability. Alice and Bob announced a 160-fold improvement in error correction using qubit squeezing applied to their cat qubits, the sa...
